enrichment.short_description: Scribus is a free, open-source desktop publishing app for professional page layout, typesetting, and print-ready PDFs.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Scribus is free, open-source desktop publishing software for page layout, CMYK printing, and interactive PDFs on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

features.title: CMYK & ICC color management
features.description: Built-in support for CMYK color and ICC color profiles for professional print output.

features.title: Python scripting
features.description: A built-in Python scripting engine for automating layout tasks.

features.title: Wide format import
features.description: Imports major bitmap formats (TIFF, JPEG, PSD) and vector formats (EPS, SVG, Adobe Illustrator files).

features.title: Advanced typography
features.description: Recent versions (1.6.0+) add footnotes, endnotes, text variables, cross-references, and orphan/widow control.

features.title: Interactive & animated PDFs
features.description: Can produce animated and interactive PDF documents in addition to standard print-ready files.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: The application interface is translated into 60 languages.

pros: Completely free and open source
Runs natively on Linux/Unix, macOS, Windows, and BSD variants
Professional print features like CMYK and ICC color management
Built-in Python scripting for automation
Translated into 60 languages
cons: Steeper learning curve than mainstream commercial DTP tools
No official cloud storage or real-time collaboration features
Interface feels dated compared to modern design apps

faqs.question: Is Scribus free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scribus is free and open-source software with no paid tier.

faqs.question: What license does Scribus use?
faqs.answer: It is released under the GNU LGPL 2.1, with some components under MIT, 3-clause BSD, and public domain licenses.

faqs.question: What platforms does Scribus support?
faqs.answer: Linux/Unix, macOS, Windows, BSD variants, and OS/2/ArcaOS, with support varying by version.

faqs.question: Can Scribus create interactive PDFs?
faqs.answer: Yes, per its own documentation it supports animated and interactive PDF creation.

use_cases.title: Print publication layout
use_cases.description: Laying out books, magazines, and brochures for professional printing with CMYK and ICC color accuracy.

use_cases.title: Open-source alternative to InDesign
use_cases.description: A no-cost desktop publishing option for teams that can't justify a commercial DTP license.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Scribus?
paragraphs: Scribus is a free and open-source desktop publishing (DTP) application used for page layout, typesetting, and preparing files for professional printing.
It supports CMYK color, ICC color management, and a wide range of import formats, and includes a built-in Python scripting engine for automating repetitive layout work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Scribus is for
paragraphs: Scribus suits independent publishers, print designers, and organizations that need professional page-layout capabilities without a commercial software subscription.

enrichment.short_description: Pencil Project is a free, open-source GUI prototyping tool for building wireframes and UI mockups on desktop.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pencil Project is a free, open-source GUI prototyping and wireframing tool with stencil libraries for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

features.title: Stencil libraries
features.description: Ships with stencil collections for frameworks and design systems such as Android, Bootstrap, and Material Design.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Available on GNU/Linux, macOS (OS X), and Windows.

features.title: Community stencil packs
features.description: Extensible through community-contributed stencils for additional UI frameworks.

features.title: GUI prototyping and mockups
features.description: Provides a desktop GUI for building wireframes and interactive mockups.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier
Open source under GPLv2
Extensible through community stencil packs
Cross-platform support for Linux, macOS, and Windows
cons: Latest stable release (3.1.1) dates to January 2023, indicating a slow development pace
Fewer real-time collaboration or cloud features than newer prototyping tools
Development roadmap for Pencil V3 has lagged its own stated timeline

faqs.question: Is Pencil Project free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source, released under GPLv2.

faqs.question: Is Pencil Project actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Its GitHub repository remains open (not archived), but the latest stable release, 3.1.1, was published in January 2023.

faqs.question: What platforms does Pencil Project support?
faqs.answer: It runs on GNU/Linux, macOS, and Windows as a desktop application.

use_cases.title: Rapid UI wireframing
use_cases.description: Sketching interactive wireframes and mockups using built-in and community stencils.
use_cases.audience: Developers and designers prototyping desktop or web UIs

use_cases.title: Teaching UX/UI design
use_cases.description: A no-cost tool for learning prototyping fundamentals.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Pencil Project?
paragraphs: Pencil Project is a free and open-source GUI prototyping tool for creating mockups and wireframes on desktop platforms, maintained by Evolus.
It includes built-in and community-contributed stencil libraries for frameworks like Bootstrap, Material Design, and Android.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Pencil Project is for
paragraphs: It suits developers, designers, and students who want a free, no-account desktop wireframing tool rather than a paid cloud prototyping suite.

enrichment.short_description: Vectr is a browser-based vector graphics editor with AI-assisted tools for creating logos, icons, and illustrations.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vectr is an online vector graphics editor with AI logo, background-removal, and image-to-vector tools plus real-time collaboration.

features.title: AI-powered tools
features.description: Includes background removal, JPG/PNG-to-SVG conversion, text-to-image generation, and logo creation.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Live workspace sharing with an integrated chat for team projects.

features.title: Cross-device cloud sync
features.description: Designs sync across devices via the cloud.

features.title: Multi-format export
features.description: Exports to SVG, PNG, JPG, EPS, AI, PDF, and SVGZ.

features.title: Browser-based editor
features.description: Runs in the browser, with Chrome noted as most compatible.

pros: Runs entirely in the browser with no install required
Includes AI-assisted design tools
Real-time multi-user collaboration
Large built-in asset library (23,000+ design elements per Vectr's site)
cons: No longer offers a permanently free plan; a paid subscription is required
AI features are metered by credits rather than unlimited
Some export formats (EPS, AI, PDF, SVGZ) may require credits

faqs.question: Is Vectr free?
faqs.answer: No. Vectr currently operates on paid Plus and Premium subscription plans rather than a free-forever tier.

faqs.question: What does the Plus plan include?
faqs.answer: 1 GB of storage, AI credits, access to 23,000+ design elements, and single-session access, according to Vectr's pricing page.

faqs.question: What export formats does Vectr support?
faqs.answer: SVG, PNG, JPG, EPS, AI, PDF, and SVGZ.

use_cases.title: Quick logo and icon design
use_cases.description: Creating logos, icons, and simple graphics directly in the browser.

use_cases.title: Team vector design collaboration
use_cases.description: Co-editing vector graphics in real time with a shared workspace.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Vectr?
paragraphs: Vectr is a cloud-based vector graphics editor for creating and converting logos, icons, and illustrations directly in the browser.
It combines a standard SVG editor (shapes, paths, text) with AI-powered tools such as background removal and image-to-vector conversion, plus real-time collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Vectr is for
paragraphs: It targets small businesses, freelancers, and small design teams who want a browser-based vector editor with AI assistance rather than installing desktop software.

enrichment.short_description: Boxy SVG is a proprietary vector graphics editor for creating SVG illustrations, icons, and logos, offered as web and desktop apps.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boxy SVG is a vector graphics editor for SVG illustrations, logos, and icons, with direct XML editing and apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, and ChromeOS.

features.title: Direct XML editing
features.description: An Elements panel allows editing the underlying SVG/XML markup directly.

features.title: Bitmap-to-vector tracing
features.description: Converts raster images into vector shapes.

features.title: SVG filter effects
features.description: Full SVG filter effects support with predefined presets.

features.title: Design service integrations
features.description: Built-in access to Google Fonts, Pixabay, and Color Hunt.

features.title: Procedural shape tools
features.description: Includes procedural shapes like cogwheels and crosses alongside standard geometric tools.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Available as a web app and as native desktop apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, and ChromeOS.

pros: Deep, standards-compliant SVG/XML editing not common in mainstream vector tools
Works as both a web app and a native desktop app across four platforms
Built-in font, stock image, and color-palette integrations
In active development since its first release in March 2013
cons: Proprietary software, not open source
Requires payment after the free trial period
Smaller plugin/community ecosystem than Adobe Illustrator or Inkscape

faqs.question: Is Boxy SVG free?
faqs.answer: Boxy SVG offers a free trial; continued use requires a paid license or subscription.

faqs.question: What platforms does Boxy SVG run on?
faqs.answer: It's available as a web app and as desktop apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, and ChromeOS.

faqs.question: Who created Boxy SVG?
faqs.answer: It was created by developer Jarosław Foksa, with a first release in March 2013.

use_cases.title: Editing raw SVG code visually
use_cases.description: Working directly with SVG markup through a visual editor and XML panel.

use_cases.title: Creating logos and illustrations
use_cases.description: Building vector logos, icons, and illustrations with procedural shape tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Boxy SVG?
paragraphs: Boxy SVG is a proprietary vector graphics editor focused on SVG file creation and editing, offering both a web app and native desktop apps.
It combines standard vector drawing tools with direct XML/markup editing, bitmap tracing, and filter-effects support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Boxy SVG is for
paragraphs: It suits web developers and designers who need precise control over SVG markup alongside a visual vector editing experience.

enrichment.short_description: Excalidraw is a free, open-source virtual whiteboard for hand-drawn style diagrams and wireframes with real-time collaboration.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Excalidraw is a free, open-source hand-drawn style whiteboard for diagrams and wireframes, with real-time collaboration under the MIT license.

features.title: Hand-drawn style canvas
features.description: An infinite canvas with a distinctive hand-drawn visual style for diagrams and sketches.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: End-to-end encrypted collaborative editing via shareable links.

features.title: Flexible export
features.description: Export to PNG, SVG, clipboard, or the native .excalidraw JSON format.

features.title: Offline PWA support
features.description: Installable as a Progressive Web App with local-first autosave.

features.title: Generative AI tools
features.description: Includes text-to-diagram and wireframe-to-code AI features.

features.title: Shape libraries & dark mode
features.description: Supports importable shape libraries and a dark mode interface.

pros: MIT-licensed and fully open source
No account required to start drawing
End-to-end encrypted real-time collaboration
Used by companies including Google Cloud, Meta, Notion, and Replit, per Excalidraw's own site
cons: Advanced team, presentation, and cloud-workspace features require the paid Excalidraw+ tier
The free version relies on browser local storage, which can be cleared unexpectedly if drawings aren't exported

faqs.question: Is Excalidraw free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Excalidraw editor is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What is Excalidraw+?
faqs.answer: A paid tier adding extended team collaboration, live presentations, cloud workspace management, and upgraded generative AI, with a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: How much does Excalidraw+ cost?
faqs.answer: $7/month billed monthly, or $6/month billed annually, according to its pricing page.

faqs.question: Does Excalidraw require an account?
faqs.answer: No, the free web app can be used without signing up.

use_cases.title: Quick collaborative sketches and wireframes
use_cases.description: Sketching diagrams and wireframes together in real time.

use_cases.title: Embedding whiteboarding in other products
use_cases.description: Using the open-source Excalidraw library to add drawing canvases to other apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Excalidraw?
paragraphs: Excalidraw is a free, open-source virtual whiteboard for creating diagrams, wireframes, and sketches with a distinctive hand-drawn look.
It supports real-time, end-to-end encrypted collaboration and can be extended through a paid Excalidraw+ tier for teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Excalidraw is for
paragraphs: It suits individuals and teams who want a lightweight, no-account whiteboard for quick diagrams, plus developers who embed its open-source library into their own products.

enrichment.short_description: tldraw is a free, instant collaborative online whiteboard, built on an infinite-canvas engine also licensable as an SDK.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: tldraw is a free, instant collaborative online whiteboard; its infinite-canvas engine is also available as an SDK for developers.

features.title: Free collaborative whiteboard
features.description: An instant, free whiteboard usable directly at tldraw.com.

features.title: Infinite canvas engine
features.description: A feature-complete infinite canvas engine underlies the product.

features.title: tldraw SDK
features.description: The underlying engine is available separately as an SDK for developers to build custom canvas apps.

features.title: Multiplayer sync
features.description: Real-time multiplayer collaboration via the @tldraw/sync package, which supports self-hosted syncing.

features.title: Embedded content support
features.description: Can embed content from sources like YouTube, Figma, and GitHub.

pros: Free to use directly as a whiteboard at tldraw.com
Highly extensible SDK for developers building custom canvas tools, shapes, and bindings
Real-time multiplayer collaboration
Adopted by companies including Google, Shopify, and Autodesk to build canvas features, per tldraw's own site
cons: The underlying SDK is source-available rather than fully open source — production use requires a paid commercial license or a free 'Hobby' license that must display a watermark
Commercial SDK licensing pricing is custom-quoted and not publicly listed

faqs.question: Is tldraw free?
faqs.answer: The hosted whiteboard at tldraw.com is free to use. The separate tldraw SDK is free for development and non-commercial 'Hobby' use (with a watermark) but requires a paid commercial license for production business use.

faqs.question: Is the tldraw SDK open source?
faqs.answer: No. It is source-available under a custom license that restricts production deployment without a license key.

faqs.question: Can tldraw's sync be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SDK's @tldraw/sync package supports self-hosted real-time syncing, according to tldraw's documentation.

use_cases.title: Quick online whiteboarding
use_cases.description: Sketching and diagramming together in a free browser-based whiteboard.

use_cases.title: Embedding a canvas editor into a product
use_cases.description: Licensing the tldraw SDK to add an infinite-canvas editor to a commercial application.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is tldraw?
paragraphs: tldraw is a free, instant collaborative online whiteboard for sketching and diagramming.
Its underlying infinite-canvas engine is also distributed as the tldraw SDK, a source-available library developers can license to build their own canvas applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who tldraw is for
paragraphs: It suits teams that want a free instant whiteboard, and developers who license the SDK to embed canvas functionality into their own products.

enrichment.short_description: WBO (Whiteboard) is a free, open-source real-time collaborative online whiteboard that can be used publicly or self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WBO is a free, open-source AGPL-licensed collaborative online whiteboard with public, private, and custom-named boards.

features.title: Real-time synchronization
features.description: All connected participants see drawing updates synchronized live.

features.title: Persistent boards
features.description: Board state is automatically saved.

features.title: Public shared board
features.description: A public workspace supports anonymous collaboration.

features.title: Private boards
features.description: Private boards use random or custom-named URLs for restricted sharing.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Available in 20+ languages.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Source code is available on GitHub for self-hosting.

pros: Completely free and open source (AGPL license)
No account or install required — works in any browser
Can be self-hosted for private or internal use
Supports 20+ languages
cons: The public board is open to anonymous users, so it isn't suited for sensitive content without using a private board
Feature set is limited compared to full design/whiteboard suites
No official native mobile app

faqs.question: Is WBO free?
faqs.answer: Yes, WBO is free and open-source software released under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host WBO?
faqs.answer: Yes, its source code is available on GitHub for self-hosting.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use WBO?
faqs.answer: No, boards can be created and shared via URL without an account.

use_cases.title: Quick anonymous sketching sessions
use_cases.description: Sharing a board URL for spontaneous, no-signup collaborative drawing.

use_cases.title: Private team whiteboarding
use_cases.description: Using private or self-hosted boards for internal collaboration.
use_cases.audience: Small teams needing a lightweight self-hosted whiteboard

long_form_blocks.heading: What is WBO?
paragraphs: WBO is a free, open-source online whiteboard that lets multiple users draw together in real time on a shared canvas.
It offers a public anonymous board as well as private boards with random or custom URLs, and its source code is available for self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who WBO is for
paragraphs: It suits teachers, remote teams, and anyone who wants a no-signup collaborative whiteboard, plus organizations that prefer to self-host it.

enrichment.short_description: Drawpile is a free, open-source app for real-time collaborative drawing, painting, and animation on a shared canvas.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Drawpile is a free, open-source GPLv3 collaborative drawing app for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and the web.

features.title: Real-time collaborative canvas
features.description: Multiple people can draw, sketch, paint, and animate on the same canvas simultaneously.

features.title: Autorecovery
features.description: Automatically recovers work after an unexpected exit.

features.title: Timelapse export
features.description: Can create timelapse/speedpaint videos of a drawing session.

features.title: Animation tools
features.description: Includes timeline-based animation features.

features.title: Brush stabilizer
features.description: Offers brush stroke previews and stabilizer adjustments.

features.title: Self-hostable server
features.description: Includes a dedicated server component (drawpile-srv) for self-hosting sessions.

pros: Free and open source under the GPLv3 license
Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and via browser
Can run a self-hosted server for private sessions
Includes animation and timelapse export tools
cons: No native iOS app
Real-time drawing requires a stable connection to a session server
Smaller plugin/brush ecosystem than commercial tools like Procreate

faqs.question: Is Drawpile free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drawpile is free and open source under the GPLv3 license.

faqs.question: What platforms does Drawpile support?
faqs.answer: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and a browser-based web version.

faqs.question: Can I host my own Drawpile server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drawpile includes a dedicated server component (drawpile-srv) for self-hosting sessions.

use_cases.title: Collaborative digital painting sessions
use_cases.description: Drawing and painting together in real time on a shared canvas.

use_cases.title: Teaching or streaming live drawing
use_cases.description: Using the shared canvas and timelapse export for tutorials or streams.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Drawpile?
paragraphs: Drawpile is a free, open-source collaborative drawing program that lets multiple people draw, sketch, paint, and animate on the same canvas at the same time.
It's available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and through a web browser, and includes a self-hostable dedicated server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Drawpile is for
paragraphs: It suits artists and groups who want to paint or sketch together live, as well as educators and streamers demonstrating drawing techniques.

enrichment.short_description: diagrams.net (draw.io) is a free, open-source diagramming tool for flowcharts, org charts, and UML, with no sign-up required.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: draw.io (diagrams.net) is a free, open-source diagramming app with real-time collaboration and integrations for Confluence, Jira, and Google Drive.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Collaborative editing with shared cursors.

features.title: Bring-your-own storage
features.description: Diagrams can be stored in Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, or locally; draw.io states it cannot access that data.

features.title: Productivity tool integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Confluence, Jira, GitHub, VS Code, Microsoft Teams, and Notion.

features.title: Desktop application
features.description: Available as a desktop app in addition to the web app.

features.title: Long-term backward compatibility
features.description: Diagrams created since 2005 continue to open in the current app.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: An official Docker image (jgraph/drawio) lets teams run draw.io entirely on their own infrastructure.

pros: Completely free with no sign-up required
Open source under the Apache 2.0 license
Deep integrations with Atlassian, Microsoft, and Google productivity tools
Can be fully self-hosted via an official Docker image
Users control where diagram data is stored
cons: The Confluence/Jira marketplace app is a separately licensed Atlassian add-on rather than part of the free base app
Interface polish can feel less refined than dedicated paid diagramming tools for advanced enterprise workflows

faqs.question: Is diagrams.net free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its homepage states it is 'Free forever' and open source, with no sign-up required.

faqs.question: What license is draw.io released under?
faqs.answer: Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: Where is my diagram data stored?
faqs.answer: Users choose their own storage — Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, or a local device — and draw.io states it cannot access that data.

faqs.question: Does draw.io integrate with Confluence and Jira?
faqs.answer: Yes, alongside Google Drive, Microsoft 365, GitHub, VS Code, and Notion.

faqs.question: Can draw.io be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, an official Docker image (jgraph/drawio) is available for running it on your own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Flowcharts and org charts
use_cases.description: Building flowcharts, org charts, and network diagrams for business documentation.

use_cases.title: Embedding diagrams in team docs
use_cases.description: Inserting live diagrams inside Confluence, Jira, or Notion pages via native integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is diagrams.net?
paragraphs: diagrams.net, also known as draw.io, is a free and open-source diagramming application for flowcharts, org charts, network diagrams, and UML.
It emphasizes data privacy by letting users store diagrams in their own Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, or local storage, and integrates natively with tools like Confluence, Jira, and GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who diagrams.net is for
paragraphs: It suits teams and individuals who need a free, privacy-conscious diagramming tool that plugs directly into their existing documentation and project-management tools.

enrichment.short_description: PlantUML is a free, open-source tool that generates UML and other diagrams from simple text descriptions.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PlantUML is a free, open-source text-to-diagram tool for UML, Gantt charts, and mind maps, offered under several open-source licenses.

features.title: Text-based diagramming
features.description: Diagrams are generated from simple textual descriptions instead of manual drag-and-drop drawing.

features.title: 9 standard UML diagram types
features.description: Sequence, use case, class, activity, component, deployment, state, object, and timing diagrams.

features.title: 15+ non-UML diagram types
features.description: Also generates Gantt charts, mind maps, network diagrams, wireframes, and more.

features.title: Multiple layout engines
features.description: Supports Graphviz, Smetana, VizJs, and ELK for diagram layout.

features.title: Flexible export
features.description: Exports to PNG, SVG, LaTeX, EPS, and ASCII art.

features.title: Documentation integrations
features.description: Integrates with tools like DokuWiki, AsciiDoc, and Markdown, plus browser extensions for GitHub.

pros: Completely free, released under a choice of open-source licenses (GPL, LGPL, MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD, EPL)
Diagrams are defined as plain text, making them version-control friendly
Broad diagram-type support well beyond standard UML
Can be self-hosted via a local server/jar file or used through an online server
cons: Requires learning PlantUML's own text syntax rather than drag-and-drop drawing
The default Graphviz-based renderer can require extra setup and dependencies
Visual styling is more limited than dedicated drag-and-drop diagram tools

faqs.question: Is PlantUML free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PlantUML is free and open source, offered under several license options including GPL, LGPL, MIT, and Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: What kinds of diagrams can PlantUML create?
faqs.answer: 9 standard UML diagram types plus 15+ non-UML diagrams such as Gantt charts, mind maps, and network diagrams.

faqs.question: Can PlantUML be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can run as a local server or jar file in addition to using PlantUML's hosted online server.

use_cases.title: Documentation-as-code diagramming
use_cases.description: Keeping architecture and sequence diagrams as version-controlled text alongside source code.

use_cases.title: Technical documentation diagrams
use_cases.description: Generating UML and Gantt-style diagrams for technical documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is PlantUML?
paragraphs: PlantUML is a free, open-source tool that turns simple text descriptions into UML and other diagram types, including sequence, class, and activity diagrams.
It also supports many non-UML diagram formats such as Gantt charts, mind maps, and network diagrams, and can be self-hosted or used via its online server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who PlantUML is for
paragraphs: It suits software engineers and technical writers who prefer defining diagrams as text — for example, to keep them under version control alongside code — rather than drawing them manually.
